title: The rollback that rolled nothing back

mcq.question_md: A ticket-booking method runs two updates — subtract a seat, insert a payment. The second one throws, and the catch block calls `con.rollback()` — yet the seat count in the database still went down. What's the most likely cause?
options: rollback() only works on SELECT statements
Auto-commit was still on, so the first update was permanently saved the moment it ran
MySQL doesn't support transactions at all
The rollback needed to be called before the exception, not after
mcq.explanation_md: By default JDBC runs with **auto-commit on**: every statement commits permanently the instant it executes. So the seat update was already carved in stone before the payment insert failed — there was nothing pending for `rollback()` to undo. The fix: `con.setAutoCommit(false)` first, then both updates, then `commit()` at the end of the try, with `rollback()` in the catch. The tempting wrong answer blames MySQL — its default InnoDB tables support transactions fine; the setting in *your code* was the problem. And rolling back 'before the exception' makes no sense — you can't undo a failure you haven't seen.
